title: Quantum battery optimized by parametric amplification
authors: Fang-Mei Yang, Jun-Hong An, Fu-Quan Dou
abstract: The parametric amplification enabled by two-photon driving constitutes a versatile platform for advanced quantum technologies. We present an optimized scheme for implementing quantum batteries (QBs) based on a superconducting circuit system, where a two-photon-driven LC resonator serves as the charger and an array of transmon qubits functions as the battery. Our results show that two-photon parametric driving exponentially enhances the effective cavity-qubit coupling, which in turn gives rise to near-degenerate energy-level structures and highly entangled quantum states. This significantly enhances the charging power and enables rapid energy transfer from the charger to the battery. Moreover, the engineered squeezed cavity mode and the associated quantum correlations effectively suppress environmentally induced decoherence, thereby delaying energy leakage and facilitating stable energy storage. The proposed scheme remains robust against practical experimental imperfections, such as parameter disorder and environmental noise, preserving its performance advantages. The work provides a feasible platform for realizing high-power, high-stability QBs and highlights the potential of parametric control in quantum energy technologies.
summary: This paper proposes a method to optimize quantum batteries using two-photon parametric amplification in superconducting circuits. By using a squeezed cavity mode, the researchers show that charging power can be exponentially enhanced and the battery can be made more robust against environmental noise. This provides a scalable platform for high-power, stable quantum energy storage.
main_problem: Optimizing the performance of quantum batteries (QBs) by balancing the trade-off between high charging power and environmental decoherence/energy leakage.
main_result: Two-photon parametric driving exponentially enhances the effective cavity-qubit coupling, significantly increasing maximum charging power while providing stability against noise through squeezed cavity modes.
method: The authors use a Lagrangian approach and unitary transformations (squeezing and rotating frames) to derive an effective generalized Dicke model, then solve the Lindblad master equation for open-system dynamics.
model_or_system: A superconducting circuit system consisting of a two-photon-driven LC resonator (the charger) coupled to an array of transmon qubits (the battery), modeled via a generalized Dicke Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Stored energy (E), average charging power (P), maximum charging power (Pmax), steady-state energy, and Wigner functions.
important_parameters: Squeezing parameter (r), coupling strength (g), cavity/qubit detunings, dissipation rates (kappa, gamma), and number of qubits (N).
main_assumptions: Transmon qubits are treated as two-level systems; the Lagrangian is truncated to the fourth order; the system is analyzed in the context of strong and ultrastrong coupling regimes.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the circuit schematic; Fig 2 displays energy spectra and Wigner functions; Fig 3 shows energy/power evolution and scaling in closed systems; Fig 4 shows energy/power evolution and scaling in open systems under dissipation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the quantum battery problem, describes the superconducting circuit model and Hamiltonian derivation, analyzes the closed-system dynamics and scaling, investigates the open-system stability and dissipation effects, and concludes with the robustness of the scheme.
why_interesting: It demonstrates how parametric control and squeezed light can be used to engineer light-matter interactions to achieve exponential gains in power and enhanced protection against decoherence in many-body quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The schematic illustrates a superconducting cir- cuit architecture consisting of an LC resonator, with induc- tance L and capacitance C, capacitively coupled to an array of N transmon qubits. Each qubit is modeled as a capacitor Cq in parallel with a Josephson junction characterized by energy EJq and capacitance CJq. The capacitive coupling is realized via the same capacitors Cj. A flux-pumped superconducting quantum interference device, formed by two Josephson junc- tions each with energy EJ and capacitance CJ, is threaded by an external magnetic flux Φext and embedded in the res- onator loop to provide a tunable nonlinear inductive element.
